title_en: The Creation of Adam and the Beginning of Human History
title_id: Penciptaan Adam dan Awal Sejarah Manusia
content_id:
Penciptaan Adam, manusia pertama, menandai transisi dalam narasi Ibn Kathir dari asal-usul kosmis menuju sejarah umat manusia. Allah menciptakan Adam dari tanah liat, dan Al-Quran menetapkan bahwa tanah liat ini melewati beberapa tahapan: ia digambarkan pada berbagai momen sebagai tanah (turab), tanah liat (thin), tanah liat yang kering (shalsal), dan tanah liat yang mengeras seperti tembikar. Ibn Kathir menyajikan deskripsi-deskripsi ini sebagai indikasi tahapan-tahapan berturut-turut dalam pembentukan wujud Adam sebelum ruh ditiupkan ke dalamnya, dengan mengandalkan tradisi tafsir untuk merekonsiliasi perbedaan-perbedaan yang tampak di antara ayat-ayat Al-Quran. Ruh kemudian ditiupkan ke dalam Adam atas perintah ilahi, dan ia menjadi manusia yang hidup yang dianugerahi dengan kemampuan berbicara, ilmu, dan kapasitas untuk beribadah. Allah mengajarkan Adam nama-nama segala sesuatu dan mendemonstrasikan pengetahuannya di hadapan para malaikat, menetapkan sejak awal kedudukan mulia manusia di antara makhluk-makhluk Allah.

Perintah kepada para malaikat untuk bersujud kepada Adam dan penolakan Iblis merupakan salah satu momen yang paling penting secara teologis dalam sejarah awal Ibn Kathir. Beliau menyajikan kisah-kisah Al-Quran secara terperinci: semua malaikat bersujud dalam ketaatan kepada perintah Allah, sementara Iblis, yang telah berada di antara mereka dalam kedekatan meski bukan dari sifat mereka, menolak karena kesombongan dan keangkuhan, mengklaim keunggulan atas Adam karena dia diciptakan dari api sementara Adam diciptakan dari tanah liat. Ibn Kathir menerapkan posisi teologis Athari bahwa Iblis berasal dari golongan jin, bukan malaikat, yang membuat penolakannya dapat dipahami dalam hal kapasitas moral untuk ketidaktaatan yang membedakan jin dari malaikat. Pertukaran antara Allah dan Iblis yang tercatat dalam Surah al-A'raf dan Surah al-Hijr dikutip sepenuhnya, termasuk deklarasi permusuhan Iblis terhadap umat manusia dan permintaannya untuk ditangguhkan hingga Hari Kebangkitan, yang Allah kabulkan sebagai ujian bagi manusia.

Masuknya Adam dan Hawwa ke dalam Surga, godaan mereka dan turunnya mereka ke bumi, disajikan dengan perhatian cermat pada teks Al-Quran maupun riwayat-riwayat tambahan. Ibn Kathir mencatat bahwa Surga tempat tinggal Adam dan Hawwa dipahami oleh mayoritas ulama sebagai Surga surgawi itu sendiri, sementara sebagian kecil berpendapat itu adalah kebun di bumi. Beliau mempertimbangkan bukti-bukti tekstual dan mendukung posisi arus utama. Pohon yang dilarang tidak disebutkan secara spesifik dalam Al-Quran, dan Ibn Kathir memperingatkan agar tidak mengandalkan Isra'iliyyat yang mengidentifikasinya sebagai gandum atau pohon ara, meskipun beliau mencatat pandangan-pandangan ini. Iblis mendekati mereka melalui penipuan, bersumpah bahwa dia adalah penasihat yang tulus, dan mereka memakan buah dari pohon itu dan menjadi sadar akan ketelanjangan mereka. Taubat Adam dan Hawwa yang kemudian disajikan sebagai sebuah teladan: mereka mengakui kesalahan mereka tanpa keputusasaan, berpaling kepada Allah dengan ucapan 'Wahai Tuhan kami, kami telah menzalimi diri kami sendiri,' dan Allah menerima taubat mereka dan membimbing mereka.

Ibn Kathir menguraikan beberapa dimensi teologis dari narasi fondasi ini. Turunnya Adam dan Hawwa ke bumi bukanlah suatu hukuman dalam arti penolakan ilahi melainkan pemenuhan rencana ilahi asli yang diumumkan kepada para malaikat, bahwa Allah akan menempatkan seorang khalifah di bumi. Dosa Adam dan Hawwa menunjukkan doktrin bahwa semua manusia memerlukan ampunan dan bimbingan ilahi, sebuah doktrin yang berlawanan dengan pembacaan teologis Kristen atas narasi yang sama. Adam kemudian menerima wahyu dari Allah, diajarkan taubat dan ibadah, dan menjadi nabi pertama dari umat manusia, menetapkan rantai kenabian yang tak terputus yang akan berakhir pada Nabi Muhammad shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam. Rantai bimbingan kenabian ini, menghubungkan penciptaan itu sendiri dengan pesan terakhir, memberikan benang narasi utama al-Bidayah sejak halaman-halaman pertamanya.
content_en:
The creation of Adam, the first human being, marks the transition in Ibn Kathir's narrative from cosmic origins to the history of humanity. Allah created Adam from clay, and the Quran specifies that this clay passed through several stages: it is described at different points as earth (turab), clay (tin), dried clay (salsal), and hardened clay like pottery. Ibn Kathir presents these descriptions as indicating successive stages in the fashioning of Adam's form before the spirit was breathed into him, drawing on the tafsir tradition to reconcile the apparent differences between Quranic verses. The spirit was then breathed into Adam by divine command, and he became a living human being endowed with speech, knowledge, and the capacity for worship. Allah taught Adam the names of all things and demonstrated his knowledge before the angels, establishing from the beginning the honored status of human beings among the creatures of Allah.

The command to the angels to prostrate before Adam and the refusal of Iblis constitute one of the most theologically significant moments in Ibn Kathir's early history. He presents the Quranic accounts in detail: all the angels prostrated in obedience to Allah's command, while Iblis, who had been among them in proximity though not of their nature, refused out of pride and arrogance, claiming superiority over Adam because he was created from fire while Adam was created from clay. Ibn Kathir applies the Athari theological position that Iblis was from among the jinn, not an angel, which makes his refusal comprehensible in terms of the moral capacity for disobedience that distinguishes jinn from angels. The exchange between Allah and Iblis recorded in Surah al-A'raf and Surah al-Hijr is cited fully, including Iblis's declaration of enmity toward humanity and his request for respite until the Day of Resurrection, which Allah granted as a test for human beings.

The entry of Adam and Hawwa' into Paradise, their temptation and subsequent descent to earth, is presented with careful attention to both the Quranic text and the supplementary narrations. Ibn Kathir notes that the Paradise in which Adam and Hawwa' resided is understood by the majority of scholars to be the heavenly Paradise itself, while a minority held it was a garden on earth. He considers the textual evidence and favors the mainstream position. The forbidden tree is not specified in the Quran, and Ibn Kathir cautions against relying on Isra'iliyyat that identify it as wheat or a fig tree, though he records these views. Iblis approached them through deception, swearing that he was their sincere adviser, and they ate from the tree and became aware of their nakedness. The subsequent repentance of Adam and Hawwa' is presented as a model: they acknowledged their wrongdoing without despair, turned to Allah with the words 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves,' and Allah accepted their repentance and guided them.

Ibn Kathir draws out several theological dimensions from this foundational narrative. The descent of Adam and Hawwa' to earth was not a punishment in the sense of divine rejection but a fulfillment of the original divine plan announced to the angels, that Allah would place a vicegerent (khalifah) in the earth. The sin of Adam and Hawwa' demonstrated the doctrine that all human beings are in need of divine forgiveness and guidance, a doctrine that stands in contrast to Christian theological readings of the same narrative. Adam subsequently received revelation from Allah, was taught repentance and worship, and became the first prophet of the human race, establishing the unbroken chain of prophecy that would culminate in Muhammad, peace be upon him. This chain of prophetic guidance, connecting creation itself to the final message, provides the central narrative thread of the Bidayah from its first pages.

The creation of Adam, the first human being, marks the transition in Ibn Kathir's narrative from cosmic origins to the history of humanity. الله created Adam from clay, and the Quran specifies that this clay passed through several stages: it is described at different points as earth (turab), clay (tin), dried clay (salsal), and hardened clay like pottery. Ibn Kathir presents these descriptions as indicating successive stages in the fashioning of Adam's form before the spirit was breathed into him, drawing on the tafsir tradition to reconcile the apparent differences between Quranic verses. The spirit was then breathed into Adam by divine command, and he became a living human being endowed with speech, knowledge, and the capacity for worship. الله taught Adam the names of all things and demonstrated his knowledge before the angels, establishing from the beginning the honored status of human beings among the creatures of الله.

The command to the angels to prostrate before Adam and the refusal of Iblis constitute one of the most theologically significant moments in Ibn Kathir's early history. He presents the Quranic accounts in detail: all the angels prostrated in obedience to الله's command, while Iblis, who had been among them in proximity though not of their nature, refused out of pride and arrogance, claiming superiority over Adam because he was created from fire while Adam was created from clay. Ibn Kathir applies the Athari theological position that Iblis was from among the jinn, not an angel, which makes his refusal comprehensible in terms of the moral capacity for disobedience that distinguishes jinn from angels. The exchange between الله and Iblis recorded in Surah al-A'raf and Surah al-Hijr is cited fully, including Iblis's declaration of enmity toward humanity and his request for respite until the Day of Resurrection, which الله granted as a test for human beings.

The entry of Adam and Hawwa' into Paradise, their temptation and subsequent descent to earth, is presented with careful attention to both the Quranic text and the supplementary narrations. Ibn Kathir notes that the Paradise in which Adam and Hawwa' resided is understood by the majority of scholars to be the heavenly Paradise itself, while a minority held it was a garden on earth. The forbidden tree is not specified in the Quran, and Ibn Kathir cautions against relying on Isra'iliyyat that identify it as wheat or a fig tree, though he records these views.
